- Mystery of Supersonic Airplane | Concorde Plane Crash | Dhruv Rathee: Kind: captions Language: en Hello, friends! On 25th July 2000, around 4PM, Air France flight 4590 was ready to take off. This plane was going from Paris to New York. But it wasn't a normal passenger flight. This is the flight of the Supersonic Concorde plane. An aeroplane which travels at double the speed of sound. You can compare its speed with a bullet shot from a gun. Normally, passenger flights by normal aeroplanes, take 8 hours to travel from Paris to New York. But this Supersonic Concorde aeroplane can travel this distance in a mere 3 and a half hours. It's also special because this flight was full of rich people. Flying in Concorde is considered a luxury. It is not something which can be afforded by an average person. Because a round-trip ticket between Paris and New York costs $12,000. And this was 22 years ago. Taking inflation into account, it would be around $20,000 in today's money. This flight ticket cost more than ₹1.5 million. At 4:40 PM, this flight was ready to take off, and starts speeding up on the runway. As soon as this aeroplane picks up speed on the runway, the Air Traffic Controller in the airport spot a fire under the plane. He informed the pilot. But by then the plane was already going quite fast, and by then it was too late to abort the flight. The plane couldn't be stopped. That's what the pilot told the Air Traffic Controller. Within seconds, this plane takes off, and the fire keeps building up, and soon the fire went out of control. The pilots of the plane try to level it. They switch off some engines, and it was found that the plane couldn't reach high speeds, people on the highway near the airport spot the plane from their cars. The scene was truly shocking. Within minutes, the pilots lose all control over the plane. And then the plane crashed into a hotel 15 km from Paris. In this crash, all 100 passengers of the plane, all 9 crew members, passed away. "The world's most prestigious aircraft crashes. More than 100 people are dead." This plane crash shocked the world. People couldn't believe it. How could this be? This was the most prestigious aeroplane. The top-class aeroplane in the world. The Concorde Supersonic aeroplane. How could this crash? In the history of the Supersonic Concordes, this was the first and only plane crash. Neither did any Concorde aircraft crash before it, nor did any after this. But this one plane crash altered the history of Supersonic aircraft forever. "Concorde. The paper dart jetliner, conceived jointly by Britain and France, will shrink the world and cut air journey times in half." "Supersonic transfer is going to be buffed. We are going to be able to bring the world closer together. In a true physical and time sense. "With Concorde suddenly, no two places will be more than 15 hours apart." "Concord lived a life of superlatives, and contradictions. But just a little too far ahead of her time." Friends, the word 'supersonic' is related to sound. To the speed of sound. Normally, the speed of sound at sea level is approximately 1,236 km/hr. It's approximate because the speed depends on the temperature of the wind as well. If the temperature is fluctuating, the speed of sound will also fluctuate a bit but more or less, it is around this number. The speed at which an aircraft travels can be divided into four categories. Broadly speaking, if the speed is less than the speed of sound, it is called a Subsonic aeroplane. If it is around the speed of sound, Transonic. If it is more than the speed of sound, Supersonic. And if it is more than five times the speed of sound, Hypersonic. Friends, the unit of this speed is Mach. Mach is basically the ratio of the speed of the aircraft and that of the sound. The aeroplanes that we normally fly in, the passenger aircraft, have a speed of around 900 km/hr. They fall into the category of Subsonic. The Concorde aircraft was... 'was' because the aircraft no longer exists, we'll talk about why it doesn't later in the video, had a speed of around 2,160 km/hr. It is almost around Mach 2.0 while the present-day passenger jets fly at around Mach 0.8. As a fun fact, in case you were wondering about the speed of the fastest aircraft now, it is Mach 3.3. More than 3,500 km/hr. It is the fighter jet Lockheed SR 71 Blackbird. The next version of the model is the SR 72, which is under development now, it is said that it will complete its first test flight in 2025. It is being said that its speed will be more than Mach 6.0 at 7,400 km/hr approximately. In this case, it will go beyond Supersonic and fall into the category of Hypersonic, because this is the category of speeds ranging from Mach 5.0 to Mach 10.0. Friends, do you know what is the most interesting thing about Supersonic aircraft? Whenever these aeroplanes fly, a sonic boom is generated. The planes fly faster than sound. The sound waves around normal passenger jets, curve like this. But the Supersonic speed is so fast, that the sound waves begin overlapping with each other. Due to this, a sharp sound similar to thunder is produced. As if there's a huge explosion. This is known as the sonic boom. If you are standing under a passing supersonic aeroplane, you will be able to hear this sonic boom. You can compare this to bursting a balloon with a pin. The pressure of the air inside is released in an instant which causes the loud bursting noise. The same thing happens on a bigger scale with supersonic aeroplanes. Many people confuse this with vapour clouds, such as in this photo the cloud forming around the flying aircraft. These vapour clouds do form around Supersonic aeroplanes, But they may even form around Transonic aircraft. This phenomenon occurs when the transonic aeroplane flies through moist air. When there is a lot of humidity in the air. The air pressure drops rapidly around the wings, and there is a drop in the temperature as well. The moisture in the air converts into a cloud. We can often spot this cloud vapour effect when an aeroplane breaches the sound barrier. But it is not the only scenario when it happens. Supersonic flights have an interesting history. You might think that it is a newly discovered technology, but actually, humans had broken the supersonic barrier in 1947. "Cracking the sound barrier in the X-1, marked another milestone in aviation history." On October 14th, 1947, Major Charles Yeager of the US Airforce became the first pilot to fly an aeroplane at supersonic speeds. He achieved this feat above the Mojave desert in America. After this, big businessmen and commercial airlines became interested in supersonic aircraft. They questioned why were the supersonic aircraft reserved for military purposes. They wanted to build similar flights for normal passengers. One that could travel at supersonic speeds. A lot of work was put into it for about 10 years. But in the early 1960s, in the midst of the Cold War between the US and the Soviet Union, the aerospace industry in the US, diverted its focus towards space. They were more interested in landing on the moon, in developing spacecraft, in comparison to aeroplanes. The developments in the supersonic sector shifted to Europe. France and Britain came together to sign a multi-billion dollar deal for research. "Britain and France joined forces on the project. In 1962, a formal agreement was signed between both nations, to bring Concorde into being. This one project can make both nations Europe's largest technological enterprise." On 29th November 1962, both governments presented the Concorde Agreement to the world. As per the agreement, both governments were supposed to build a supersonic jetliner. Which would be used for the passengers. Not for the military. Some years later, in March 1969, the first successful flight of the Concorde aircraft took place. "...ascension continuing, the nose has come up to 20°. She flies!" And just like that, within the next few years, Concorde became the World's first supersonic flight. Where not only military personnel, but civilians as well, could now travel at supersonic speeds. The tagline of the flight was 'Arrive Before You Leave.' Because it could cross the Atlantic Ocean from Europe to America in half the time. It is very prestigious for Concorde that even till now, it is the only commercially flying supersonic aircraft. And you might be surprised to know this, 90% of the aeroplanes built now, are built by 2 companies. Airbus and Boeing. There's almost a duopoly of these two companies Perhaps the most unique thing about these aircraft was the nose of the aircraft. The nose at the front could be adjusted by moving it up and down. During the take-offs and landings, to ensure that the pilots could see and get proper visibility, the nose could be drooped downwards. And when the aeroplane flew in the sky, it could be moved upwards for better aerodynamics. Its interior wasn't very spacious compared to the present-day planes. It could carry 100 passengers only. The interior used to be quite simple initially, eventually, with time it became more luxurious. Because the cost of the tickets for this aeroplane was not cheap. Since only the rich were purchasing its ticket, the airline decided to upgrade the interiors to be more luxurious. Eventually, things like caviar and champagne were also served on the plane. And travelling in this aeroplane had become akin to a status symbol for the people. In the 1990s, the biggest superstars in the world, celebrities, sports stars, politicians, everyone had at least one flight in the Concorde Aircraft. Even former British Prime Minister Tony Blair had travelled in the plane. During its illustrious career, Concorde had an amazing safety record. There were no problems with these flights. In more than 25 years of this plane being flown, there weren't any plane crashes. Safety was a priority here. It included luxury as well. But all of it changed on 25th July 2000. Air France flight 4590, crashed near Paris. I talked about it at the beginning of this video. All 109 people present in the plane perished in this crash. After the investigation, it was revealed that the plane that had taken off from the runway before this, had left behind 17-inch metal bit on the runway. And when this Air France flight got on the runway to take off, the small bit of the metal struck its tyre, slashing the tyre, though the tyre wasn't punctured, the small bits of the tyre, lit the underside of the left wing of the plane at high speed. This created a high-pressure shock wave. Due to this, the fuel tank burst. The plane hadn't even taken off, that the fuel had started leaking, and because there's a lot of heat there, the plane's wheels have a high rpm, and the engine was running as well, the heat from all of that caused a spark in this fuel. The Air Traffic Controller noticed this fire catching on, but it was quite late by then. The plane couldn't be stopped anymore. The plane took off and the fire kept spreading rapidly. The pilots of the plane couldn't get the fire under control. They tried to turn off the engine, the plane started slowing down, and eventually, it crashed into a hotel. As I told you, this incident shocked people from all around the world. Because till then, everyone believed that being on this plane was highly safe, it was a matter of prestige and status. It can be compared to the Titanic. People believed that the ship Titanic was unsinkable. Similarly, Concorde was a prestigious plane that couldn't crash. After this crash, the reputation of the plane was lost. Air France and British Airways decided together to temporarily retire all the Concorde flights, until they complete their investigations. A few years later, in November 2001, they reintroduced the Concorde flights. But their business couldn't recover. There were several reasons for this. The ruined reputation of the aeroplane after the plane crash, this was one of the most obvious reasons, but apart from this, in November 2001, at the time when this aeroplane was reintroduced, was a terrible period. We know what happened in September 2001, The World Trade Centre was attacked, which led to the aircraft industry coming to a halt. People were scared to travel in aeroplanes. Additionally, there were some drawbacks with the plane as well. The sonic boom I told you about, it is truly amazing when you experience it for the first time, but if you have to listen to a sonic boom above your home every day, it would turn into a nuisance for you. The people living in the path of the Supersonic aeroplane, were very bothered with this. The noise levels in the Concorde were excruciatingly high. To keep the noise levels down, the airlines decided that the flight route of Concorde, would be mainly above the oceans. Rather than above land. Even so, people living near the airports, started protesting against the Concordes. Another problem was fuel consumption. In comparison to a normal passenger aeroplane, the fuel consumption in Concorde was four times that. Neither was it good for the environment nor for the economy. And finally, we come to the last major reason the costs to maintain and operate these planes were really high. The annual cost of maintaining the Concorde fleet for British Airways, was £1 billion. That was back then, today it would be around £1.7 billion. If you look at this from the perspective of the passenger, spending ₹1.5 million on a flight ticket, just to reach quickly, but at the end of it, you'd be sitting in this seat. And it's not even a spacious seat. It is better to be spending the same money on business class or first class seats. At least you can lie down. You'd get more luxury in a sense. There wasn't a high enough demand for it, but whatever there used to be, dwindled down after the plane crash. Due to the ruined reputation, the Concorde flights started becoming emptier. Due to these reasons, in 2003, Air France and British Airways had decided that they would shut down their Concorde flights permanently. On 24th October 2003, the last commercial Concorde flight took off. It was the last supersonic flight in history. Today, almost after 20 years, no supersonic commercial flights exist. Normally, when we talk about any topic, in any sector, we see technology progressing. But this is one of the rare spots, where technology has regressed. Logically, you might wonder how this came to be. Supersonic flights should have become more common nowadays. Who doesn't want to reach sooner? Everyone wants aeroplanes to travel faster, but this is the unfortunate story. But do you know what's good about this? In the near or distant future, Supersonic aircraft will make a comeback. The drawbacks of this technology, are being worked on, with new solutions being developed. A recent startup, Boom, builds supersonic aircraft. United Airlines, a major American airline, announced that they have ordered 15 planes from this startup. The planes would travel at the speed of Mach 1.7. They have named it the Overture Aircraft. If this deal is successful, and everything goes as planned, you will see these aeroplanes back on the runway in 2029. 2029 might be the year when we'd be able to get on a supersonic aircraft once again. Environmental damage was one of the most significant negative points, to counter that, the company has claimed that they would use only sustainable aviation fuel. The ones made up of biodegradable materials. They also said that the aircraft they're building, is being manufactured using net-zero carbon emissions methods. No extra carbon dioxide is produced in this process. Keeping the environmental impact so low to make it economically feasible, and to develop the technology with time, is not going to be easy. Interestingly, even if the Overture aircraft are launched in 2029, its speed will still be less than that of the Concorde. They'd fly at Mach 1.7, in comparison to the Mach 2.0 at which the Concorde used to fly. Even after this, Concorde would remain the fast passenger jet. But even at this speed, one of the direct benefits will be that the flights from Dubai to Singapore, which take 7 hours nowadays, would take only 4 hours in these aeroplanes. - This Jet Terrified the West: The MiG-25 Foxbat: Kind: captions Language: en This video was made possible by CuriosityStream.&nbsp; Watch thousands of high-quality documentaries&nbsp;&nbsp; and get access to my streaming service,&nbsp; Nebula using the link in the description. &nbsp; In November 1971, two Israeli fighter jets&nbsp; race to intercept an unidentified aircraft.&nbsp;&nbsp; The F4 Phantoms are among the fastest jets&nbsp; in the world, but they’re not fast enough.&nbsp;&nbsp; Because they’re chasing a MiG-25. A plane&nbsp; that can fly so fast and high, it can outrun&nbsp;&nbsp; any fighter or air defense system. For nearly a decade, the Soviet Union’s&nbsp;&nbsp; MiG-25 will remain a complete mystery and leave&nbsp; western intelligence scrambling for answers. In the opening years of the Cold War a new kind&nbsp; of weapon emerged, the intercontinental bomber.&nbsp;&nbsp; An aircraft with enough range to reach nearly any&nbsp; part of the world to deliver a nuclear strike.&nbsp;&nbsp; America’s first intercontinental&nbsp;bomber not only flew further,&nbsp;&nbsp; it flew higher and faster than any bomber before&nbsp; it, making it extremely difficult to intercept.&nbsp;&nbsp; And it started a decade-long trend of&nbsp; building strategic bombers that flew&nbsp;&nbsp; ever higher and faster. The Soviet Union responded by building&nbsp;&nbsp; increasingly capable fighter-interceptors&nbsp; to try and stop them. But by the late 1950s,&nbsp;&nbsp; Soviet intelligence had learned that the Americans&nbsp; were developing a new state-of-the-art bomber that&nbsp;&nbsp; would fly so high and fast, it would render the&nbsp; entire Soviet Air Force virtually obsolete. The XB-70 was so cutting edge, that only a few&nbsp; years earlier it would have been impossible to&nbsp;&nbsp; build. But the Americans were aiming to have&nbsp; the bomber ready in just a few short years. For Soviet leadership, a&nbsp;sense of panic set in. As the largest country in the world,&nbsp; there were over twenty two million square&nbsp;&nbsp; kilometers of airspace guard. At any moment,&nbsp; American bombers could emerge from the north,&nbsp;&nbsp; or from bases in the east, or NATO&nbsp; allied countries in the west. And only a handful of supersonic bombers&nbsp; would be enough to overwhelm air defenses. The Soviet Union would have just a few short years&nbsp; to design and build a new interceptor to match the&nbsp;&nbsp; incredible performance of the upcoming B-70. It would have to be as fast as Soviet ingenuity&nbsp;&nbsp; could make it, and developed in record&nbsp; time. But to effectively guard the country’s&nbsp;&nbsp; enormous airspace, they’d also need to mass&nbsp; produce the new jet by the hundreds. The MiG-25 was the Soviet Union’s&nbsp; answer, an interceptor that would&nbsp;&nbsp; overcome the odds with brute force. To catch the XB-70, engineers would need to&nbsp;&nbsp; equip the MiG with enormously powerful engines.&nbsp; But there wouldn't be time to develop new ones.&nbsp;&nbsp; Instead, they’d make due with a turbojet&nbsp; originally built to power cruise missiles&nbsp;&nbsp; and reconnaissance drones. They were the&nbsp; largest engines ever put on a fighter,&nbsp;&nbsp; but they were essentially disposable. It meant&nbsp; that initial versions of the MiG had engines&nbsp;&nbsp; with a service life of just 150 hours. At intercept speeds , the MiG-25 would have&nbsp;&nbsp; to withstand serious kinetic heating,&nbsp; with some parts reaching 300 Celsius.&nbsp;&nbsp; But lightweight heat-resistant titanium was just&nbsp; too difficult to produce on a mass scale. So engineers built the jet largely out of heavy&nbsp; nickel-steel alloy, a material that could be&nbsp;&nbsp; quickly welded together and easily repaired at&nbsp; even the most remote and ill-equipped airbase. But it meant the more than forty thousand pound&nbsp; jet could never be maneuverable, a characteristic&nbsp;&nbsp; considered irrelevant for its mission. To spot enemy bombers, the MiG-25 was&nbsp;&nbsp; equipped with a colossal 600 kilowatt&nbsp; radar designed to detect high flying&nbsp;&nbsp; aircraft up to a hundred kilometers away and&nbsp; burn right through their jamming devices. And it would only need one kind of weapon. Four of&nbsp; the largest air-to-air missiles ever produced. Along with the interceptor, there would&nbsp; also be a dedicated reconnaissance version&nbsp;&nbsp; with powerful surveillance cameras, increased&nbsp; range, and an even higher service ceiling. Some&nbsp;&nbsp; versions could even be equipped for high altitude&nbsp; bombing. But the vast majority of MiG-25s would&nbsp;&nbsp; be built as dedicated interceptors. Mass production began in 1969,&nbsp;&nbsp; and at one point, one hundred MiG-25’s&nbsp; were rolling off assembly lines&nbsp;&nbsp; every single month. The Mig-25 would fly higher&nbsp; and faster than any combat jet in history, and it&nbsp;&nbsp; would have a profound effect on the Cold War. Just&nbsp; not in the way the Soviets were expecting. In 1967, the Soviet Union put on a&nbsp; massive airshow. And they made sure&nbsp;&nbsp; the Americans were watching. The event was even&nbsp; broadcast in English for western audiences. The Soviets unveiled several new aircraft for&nbsp; the very first time, but they saved the best for&nbsp;&nbsp; last. In the final 10 minutes, three prototype&nbsp; MiG-25s were sent roaring past the audience.&nbsp;&nbsp; It was the first time anyone outside of the&nbsp; Soviet Union would see the new aircraft. And&nbsp;&nbsp; for western intelligence, it&nbsp;set off alarm bells. The grainy footage revealed an aircraft&nbsp; with all the hallmarks of an agile fighter,&nbsp;&nbsp; the unusually large wings suggested extreme&nbsp; maneuverability. Enormous air intakes hinted&nbsp;&nbsp; at massive engines and experts suspected the&nbsp; use of advanced lightweight titanium. But what worried them the most, was that the plane&nbsp; looked eerily similar to concepts for America’s&nbsp;&nbsp; next-generation air superiority fighter. A jet&nbsp; that wouldn't be ready for another ten years. In Washington, military planners&nbsp; struggled to understand how the&nbsp;&nbsp; Soviets could have leapfrogged so far&nbsp; ahead. Because only a few months later,&nbsp;&nbsp; the Soviet Union started registering incredible&nbsp; new world records for speed and altitude. And the media soon caught wind, spreading&nbsp; fears that the mysterious new jet&nbsp;&nbsp; could outperform anything built by the West. The&nbsp; US Air Force Chief of Staff even publicly admitted&nbsp;&nbsp; that for the first time, the Soviet’s had a&nbsp; jet that the United States couldn't match. The perceived threat posed by the mysterious&nbsp; new jet motivated the US to drastically increase&nbsp;&nbsp; performance targets for its next-generation&nbsp; fighters. But for years, the Americans were&nbsp;&nbsp; desperate for any information they could get. They’d catch another glimpse in 1971,&nbsp;&nbsp; when Israeli radar controllers began&nbsp; tracking MiG-25’s over the Saini peninsula.&nbsp;&nbsp; The jets were clocked flying at more than two and&nbsp; half times the speed of sound, at an altitude of&nbsp;&nbsp; over twenty kilometers. At one point, they even&nbsp; tracked one of the MiGs accelerating beyond&nbsp;&nbsp; Mach 3. But the jets flew so high and fast,&nbsp; no fighter in the world could catch them. For over a decade, the MiG-25 remained&nbsp; a mystery and continued to worry western&nbsp;&nbsp; intelligence. But that was about to change&nbsp; in the most dramatic way possible. On September 6th, 1976, the Soviet Union’s most&nbsp; secretive jet showed up seemingly out of nowhere&nbsp;&nbsp; over northern Japan. It then made a dramatic&nbsp; crash landing at a sleepy commercial airport,&nbsp;&nbsp; Nearly colliding with an airliner and&nbsp; skidding right off the end of a runway. It was the first time anyone in the West would&nbsp; see a MiG-25 in person. And as curious onlookers&nbsp;&nbsp; gathered, no one seemed to know where&nbsp; it had come from or how it got here. Piloting the MiG was 29 year old Viktor&nbsp; Belenko. He was quickly taken into custody&nbsp;&nbsp; where he explained that he escaped the Soviet&nbsp; Union to seek asylum in the United States. Disillusioned with life in the Soviet&nbsp; Union and harsh conditions at his airbase,&nbsp;&nbsp; Belenko had planned his escape for months. While&nbsp; on a training exercise over the sea of Japan,&nbsp;&nbsp; he reported engine trouble, giving him a&nbsp; chance to fall back from his group. Belenko then descended low enough&nbsp; to evade radar detection. Once&nbsp;&nbsp; out of Soviet air space, he set course&nbsp; for the nearest Japanese air base.&nbsp;&nbsp; But with limited fuel and difficulty navigating,&nbsp; he was forced to find the nearest airport. Belenko was well aware that his&nbsp; MiG-25 was a prized possession&nbsp;&nbsp; and he provided invaluable information to&nbsp; western intelligence, who shipped his MiG to a&nbsp;&nbsp; nearby air base to examine every inch of it. And they were in for the shock of a lifetime.&nbsp;&nbsp; The MiG-25 obviously wasn’t the agile&nbsp; fighter they were expecting. Its heavy stainless steel airframe severely&nbsp; limited maneuverability and the large wings&nbsp;&nbsp; were needed just to keep the jet airborne. The&nbsp; engines, while powerful enough to exceed Mach 3,&nbsp;&nbsp; couldn’t sustain those speeds without&nbsp; permanent damage. Meaning the practical&nbsp;&nbsp; limit was around Mach 2.8. The MiG’s radar was powerful but&nbsp;&nbsp; lacked look-down capability, meaning it&nbsp; couldn't track low flying targets. The compromises that Soviet engineers were forced&nbsp; to make were now glaringly apparent. But they&nbsp;&nbsp; wouldn’t have mattered much for intercepting a&nbsp; high-altitude, supersonic bomber like the B-70.&nbsp;&nbsp; But the B-70 never made it into production.&nbsp; Instead, the Americans switched their tactics&nbsp;&nbsp; from high speed and high altitude, to low&nbsp; altitude radar and defense evading aircraft. Leaving western intelligence perplexed as to why&nbsp; the MiG-25 was produced in such large numbers.&nbsp;&nbsp; The only other aircraft the interceptor&nbsp; would potentially have to guard against&nbsp;&nbsp; were a handful of reconnaissance aircraft. The same media that once stoked fears about a&nbsp;&nbsp; Soviet super-plane now openly mocked the MiG&nbsp; as a crudely built machine that was more or&nbsp;&nbsp; less useless in combat. An inferior aircraft that&nbsp; proved the Soviets were behind in technology. Belenko’s defection outraged Soviet leadership,&nbsp; who demanded that he return to the Soviet&nbsp;&nbsp; Union along with the stolen MiG. Instead,&nbsp; Belenko was granted American citizenship,&nbsp;&nbsp; while his MiG was sent back to the&nbsp; Soviet Union in dozens of pieces. The Americans had learned everything there was&nbsp; to know about the MiG-25, and they now understood&nbsp;&nbsp; critical components of the Soviet air defense&nbsp; system. With the MiG-25 so thoroughly compromised,&nbsp;&nbsp; Soviet engineers had to work around the&nbsp; clock to modernize the aircraft. Equipping the MiG-25 with more&nbsp; powerful and reliable engines,&nbsp;&nbsp; a more sophisticated look-down radar, and the&nbsp; ability to field a wider range of missiles.. Meanwhile older versions of the jet,&nbsp; no longer a closely guarded secret,&nbsp;&nbsp; were exported to other countries. In all,&nbsp; nearly twelve hundred MiG-25s were produced. But more than fifty years later, the MiG-25&nbsp; is still the fastest fighter jet ever built.&nbsp;&nbsp; An aircraft that would set twenty nine&nbsp; World Records. In 1977, a MiG-25 climbed&nbsp;&nbsp; to an incredible altitude of 123,000 feet,&nbsp; setting a record that still stands today. Although the aircraft had been built&nbsp; to intercept high altitude bombers,&nbsp;&nbsp; downgraded export versions even&nbsp; saw moderate success in combat. But by the late 1970s, the Soviet&nbsp; Union had moved on. Because&nbsp;&nbsp; development of a next-generation interceptor&nbsp; was well underway. A jet that would have cutting&nbsp;&nbsp; edge avionics, sophisticated engines,&nbsp; and a radar and weapons control system&nbsp;&nbsp; so advanced, it could engage multiple targets&nbsp; simultaneously from a hundred kilometers away&nbsp;&nbsp; and even link with other fighters to coordinate&nbsp; an attack. In the process, instantly upgrading&nbsp;&nbsp; the capabilities of older Soviet fighters. Where the MiG-25’s design was about brute force,&nbsp;&nbsp; the new MiG-31 would use the state-of-the-art to&nbsp; become the world's most formidable interceptor. - From the Flight Deck – Van Nuys Airport (VNY): Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Each airport in the National Airspace System&nbsp; has unique and distinct characteristics.&nbsp;&nbsp; This can sometimes lead pilots to use the wrong&nbsp; surface in taxi, takeoff, or landing operations.&nbsp;&nbsp; Let's take a look at some of these&nbsp; tricky areas from the flight deck. Van Nuys is a medium-sized primarily&nbsp; business and general aviation airport&nbsp;&nbsp; located in the city of Van Nuys, California.&nbsp; It is situated six miles west of the Hollywood&nbsp;&nbsp; Burbank Airport and 15 miles north of the&nbsp; Los Angeles International Airport. [Music] Van Nuys has its own Class Delta Airspace which&nbsp; underlies the Burbank Class Charlie and is just&nbsp;&nbsp; north of the Los Angeles Class Bravo. Pilots&nbsp; intending to fly to Van Nuys need to be sure&nbsp;&nbsp; they understand the rules and requirements to&nbsp; operate in this type of environment. The traffic&nbsp;&nbsp; mix consists of everything from Gyrocopters to&nbsp; Boeing 737s and even the occasional Military C-17.&nbsp;&nbsp; The airport has long been known as one of the&nbsp; busiest GA airports in the country. In addition,&nbsp;&nbsp; it is home to no less than 15 different flight&nbsp; schools providing both Fixed and Rotor Wing&nbsp;&nbsp; instruction. This mix of pilot experience,&nbsp; wide variations of aircraft capability,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the sheer traffic volume make Van Nuys Airport&nbsp; a challenging airport for pilots and controllers.&nbsp;&nbsp; The airport has two closely spaced&nbsp; parallel runways with staggered thresholds.&nbsp;&nbsp; Runways 1-6 Left and Right and 3-4 Left and Right.&nbsp; Full length parallel taxiways on either side of&nbsp;&nbsp; the runways provide access to all facilities,&nbsp; ramps, and services located on the airport.&nbsp;&nbsp; There is no airport sponsored transient&nbsp; parking on the Van Nuys airport.&nbsp;&nbsp; Some transient parking may be available&nbsp; at the FBO'S near the fuel pit.&nbsp;&nbsp; Pilots need to contact the FBO in advance&nbsp; for availability and reservations. First-time arrivals to Van Nuys need to&nbsp; be aware of several traffic and airspace&nbsp;&nbsp; issues. Familiarity with the VFR reporting points&nbsp; found on the VFR sectional will help the tower&nbsp;&nbsp; with traffic sequencing. There are a number of&nbsp; commonly used reporting points located around&nbsp;&nbsp; Van Nuys including Cal State Northridge,&nbsp; The Va Hospital, Sepulveda Flood Basin,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Northridge Mall. If you are not familiar&nbsp; or can't find these points, advise the tower&nbsp;&nbsp; and they will provide alternate instructions.&nbsp; Four miles northeast of Van Nuys is the smaller,&nbsp;&nbsp; Whiteman airport and its associated Class Delta&nbsp; Airspace. Aircraft departing Whiteman northwest&nbsp;&nbsp; bound and clear of both the Whiteman and Van&nbsp; Nuys Class Delta Airspaces are not required to be&nbsp;&nbsp; talking to ATC. These aircraft may conflict with&nbsp; traffic inbound to 1-6 Left and Right at Van Nuys. Due to the high volume of traffic at Van Nuys and&nbsp; the proximity of Whiteman departures to the 1-6&nbsp;&nbsp; Left and Right final approach courses at Van Nuys,&nbsp; pilots need to exercise additional vigilance in&nbsp;&nbsp; this area. Due to complexity and volume issues,&nbsp; pilots can expect detailed instructions on how to&nbsp;&nbsp; approach the Van Nuys Airport. Close attention&nbsp; to and precise execution of these instructions&nbsp;&nbsp; is essential when arriving. Base to final turns&nbsp; need to be made so as to not cross the extended&nbsp;&nbsp; center line for the assigned runway as this&nbsp; could put traffic in conflict with straight in&nbsp;&nbsp; or base leg traffic for the other runway. "Final&nbsp; traffic will be off your right on base for the&nbsp;&nbsp; parallel runway two thousand indicated." Another&nbsp; unique aspect of the Van Nuys Airport is that the&nbsp;&nbsp; final approach course for the Burbank runway eight&nbsp; arrivals crosses directly overhead the airport. [Music]&nbsp;&nbsp; "Thank you you're following the traffic on&nbsp; base to final returning a disturbance to the&nbsp;&nbsp; veterans hospital number three Runway 1-6&nbsp; Left clear for the option." When given an&nbsp;&nbsp; aircraft to follow for landing, if you&nbsp; do not see the traffic tell the tower,&nbsp;&nbsp; they will give you alternate instructions to keep&nbsp; the traffic flow safe and orderly. When operating&nbsp;&nbsp; an aircraft on the surface of Van Nuys accurate&nbsp; position reports to the tower or ground control&nbsp;&nbsp; are essential. For example when clearing&nbsp; Runway 1-6 Right at Taxiway Foxtrot,&nbsp;&nbsp; if the pilot simply states Cessna 1-2-3 cleared&nbsp; Foxtrot, the calling aircraft could be at any&nbsp;&nbsp; of three locations on the airport. This type of&nbsp; ambiguity adds to controller workload and is best&nbsp;&nbsp; avoided by accurate position reports on the field&nbsp; such as, clear of Runway 1-6 right at Foxtrot,&nbsp;&nbsp; holding short Runway 1-6 Left at Charlie or&nbsp; holding in between the runways on Taxiway Foxtrot.&nbsp;&nbsp; Precise position reports help the tower and&nbsp; get you to where you want to go in the most&nbsp;&nbsp; expeditious manner. In addition, knowing&nbsp; your destination on the airport property&nbsp;&nbsp; before you land will help the tower give you&nbsp; runway exit instructions and taxi clearance.&nbsp;&nbsp; When exiting the runways pilots need to remember&nbsp; that they are not considered clear of the runway&nbsp;&nbsp; until the aircraft is completely across the&nbsp; hold short lines protecting the runway that&nbsp;&nbsp; they are exiting. When given taxi instructions&nbsp; pay close attention to route and aircraft sequence&nbsp;&nbsp; and be aware that like the surrounding airspace,&nbsp; the surface of Van Nuys can get extremely busy&nbsp;&nbsp; and present complex situations. Know that both&nbsp; the northwest and northeast corners of the airport&nbsp;&nbsp; surface are tower non-visibility areas. Because of&nbsp; this, when ready to depart Van Nuys providing the&nbsp;&nbsp; tower your location accurately will help them&nbsp; get you on your way. If you are parked in the&nbsp;&nbsp; parking area there is a sign on the outbound&nbsp; taxiway Alpha 2 that identifies checkpoint&nbsp;&nbsp; one. Pilots are requested to call for taxi&nbsp; clearance just prior to reaching this location.&nbsp;&nbsp; If requesting flight following, prior to taxi&nbsp; contact Van Nuys clearance delivery on 126.6&nbsp;&nbsp; and provide the controller with call sign,&nbsp; aircraft type, equipment suffix, altitude request,&nbsp;&nbsp; and destination. This will help the tower get you&nbsp; into the ATC system and efficiently on your way.&nbsp;&nbsp; Pilots are advised to use caution in this&nbsp; area and to be especially aware of vehicles&nbsp;&nbsp; operating on the airport vehicle roadway, which is&nbsp; within the area delineated by the zippered lines.&nbsp;&nbsp; Vehicles are instructed to give way to aircraft,&nbsp; but pilots should be aware of these vehicles and&nbsp;&nbsp; maintain their situational awareness at&nbsp; all times. As with any taxi clearance,&nbsp;&nbsp; a complete understanding of route&nbsp; turns and any hold short instructions&nbsp;&nbsp; before entering the movement area will go a&nbsp; long way in avoiding any possible conflicts or&nbsp;&nbsp; incidents. Once in the run-up area and a run-up is&nbsp; complete, contact the tower for takeoff clearance.&nbsp;&nbsp; There is no need to call ground first. It should&nbsp; be noted that aircraft that are not departing and&nbsp;&nbsp; need to return to the taxiway or ramp must contact&nbsp; ground control before leaving the run-up area. After takeoff it is essential that&nbsp; pilots fly the appropriate runway heading&nbsp;&nbsp; as instructed by the tower and not drift&nbsp; off center line. Due to traffic volume,&nbsp;&nbsp; there is likely to be another departure&nbsp; from the parallel runway in close proximity. As with almost all airports in the Los&nbsp; Angeles basin, Van Nuys is noise sensitive.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is especially true of departures from&nbsp; Runway 1-6 Left and Right. All aircraft&nbsp;&nbsp; are requested to delay any turns until they&nbsp; reach the flood basin south of the airport.&nbsp;&nbsp; As with any clearance or instruction, if you don't&nbsp; understand or are confused the easiest answer is&nbsp;&nbsp; to ask the tower. They are there to help. Being&nbsp; aware of the configuration issues at Van Nuys&nbsp;&nbsp; will help pilots make better decisions and&nbsp; along with the air traffic control tower,&nbsp;&nbsp; keep the operation safe and efficient. We&nbsp; hope this short video helps you prepare&nbsp;&nbsp; for your trip to the Van Nuys airport.&nbsp; It's always better to know before you go.

  9. @BruceAirFlying: At about the 8-minue mark, the video discusses "fly runway heading." That section may add to the confusion that many pilots have about that instruction/clearance. Under VFR, you should crab as necessary during initial climb to ensure that you don't drift over a parallel runway.

But when you're on an IFR clearance that specifies "runway heading," you should track the extended centerline until reaching at least 400 ft above the airport elevation and then fly the assigned heading. As the P/C Glossary, AIM, IPH, and other sources note, under IFR: "RUNWAY HEADING− The magnetic direction that corresponds with the runway centerline extended, not the painted runway number. When cleared to “fly or maintain runway heading,” pilots are expected to fly or maintain the heading that corresponds with the extended centerline of the departure runway. Drift correction shall not be applied; e.g., Runway 4, actual magnetic heading of the runway centerline 044, fly 044" (P/CG) and "Additionally, when required, departure instructions specify the actual heading to be flown after takeoff... “Climb via heading 112 degrees...” Some existing procedures specify, “Climb runway heading.”Over time, both of these departure instructions will be updated to read, “Climb heading 112 degrees....” Runway heading is the magnetic direction that corresponds with the runway centerline extended (charted on the airport diagram), not the numbers painted on the runway. Pilots cleared to “fly or maintain runway heading” are expected to fly or maintain the published heading that corresponds with the extended centerline of the departure runway (until otherwise instructed by ATC), and are not to apply drift correction (e.g., RWY 11, actual magnetic heading of the runway centerline 112.2 degrees, “fly heading 112 degrees”). In the event of parallel departures, this prevents a loss of separation caused by only one aircraft applying a wind drift."(IPH)

Perhaps this video should be updated to clarify that point.

- The Logistics Of A Boeing 747 Private Jet: Kind: captions Language: en-GB Private jets come in all shapes and sizes. While&nbsp; smaller aircraft, such as those from Cessna,&nbsp;&nbsp; Bombardier or Embraer, are more common, variants&nbsp; of commercial jets are also used. There has&nbsp;&nbsp; not yet been a private A380, meaning the 747 is&nbsp; currently the largest in use for such a purpose.&nbsp; But… What do these conversions look&nbsp; like, and who ends up using them?&nbsp; Before we begin, if you haven’t already, why not&nbsp; subscribe to our channel? New videos are posted&nbsp;&nbsp; weekly. And be sure to click that notification&nbsp; bell to know when the latest videos come out.&nbsp; Boeing offers its full range of commercial&nbsp; aircraft for private use through its&nbsp;&nbsp; Boeing Business Jet – or BBJ – program.&nbsp; According to the planemaker’s own data,&nbsp;&nbsp; the 737 has been the most popular by&nbsp; far, with more than 190 aircraft ordered.&nbsp;&nbsp; But the widebody 767, 777, 787, and 747 have&nbsp; all been repurposed for VIP use as well.&nbsp; As with any private jet, layout and designs are&nbsp; customized. Boeing offers the aircraft empty,&nbsp;&nbsp; and interiors are designed and&nbsp; installed by other companies.&nbsp; The 747’s upper deck has been used as a feature&nbsp; by many commercial airlines. Bars and lounges&nbsp;&nbsp; were common in the early days, with many later&nbsp; retrofitting this level with premium cabins.&nbsp;&nbsp; In private use, this area can provide a&nbsp; useful separate space away from the main deck.&nbsp; Government aircraft might use this for staff&nbsp; or secure facilities like communications.&nbsp;&nbsp; In individual use, it might feature a private&nbsp; residential space for owners or VVIPs. The 747-8 already has one of the&nbsp; highest ranges of any Boeing aircraft,&nbsp;&nbsp; although the smaller 777-200LR exceeds it, as&nbsp; will the standard 777-8 when it enters service.&nbsp;&nbsp; In a private configuration, with fewer&nbsp; fittings and carrying fewer passengers,&nbsp;&nbsp; its already substantial range can be increased. Boeing quotes the private version of the 747-8&nbsp;&nbsp; as having a range of 16,537 km (assuming a&nbsp; passenger capacity of 100). This is up from&nbsp;&nbsp; 14,320 km for the standard 747-8. At least two aircraft took this even further. The&nbsp;&nbsp; VC-25A currently used for Air Force One has the&nbsp; ability to refuel in the air. However, this has&nbsp;&nbsp; never been used in active service, and will not&nbsp; be a feature of the forthcoming VC-25B aircraft. It is hard to determine exactly how many VIP&nbsp; configured, or private 747s there have been,&nbsp;&nbsp; or are, in service. This sort of information&nbsp; is not published, and many of the earlier&nbsp;&nbsp; 747s that ended up in private use were purchased&nbsp; second-hand or managed through leasing companies.&nbsp; As a guide, Boeing stated in 2007 (as reported&nbsp; in FlightGlobal) that 22 VIP-configured 747s&nbsp;&nbsp; were in service. And at that time, it also&nbsp; had orders for four new 747-8 VIP aircraft.&nbsp; According to Boeing data, there have&nbsp; now been 10 orders for a 747-8 BBJ.&nbsp;&nbsp; All these orders were placed&nbsp; between 2006 and 2011. The most common use of VIP or private&nbsp; configured 747 is for head of state travel,&nbsp;&nbsp; or other government use. Boeing has&nbsp; seen strong success in this market,&nbsp;&nbsp; with several countries using 747s&nbsp; from the 747-100/200 generation.&nbsp; Countries that currently have used the 747 in&nbsp; their VIP or head of state fleets include the US,&nbsp;&nbsp; Bahrain, Brunei, Korea, Kuwait, Morocco,&nbsp; Oman, Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and the UAE.&nbsp; Several other countries use 747s jointly with&nbsp; state airlines. These can be converted to VIP&nbsp;&nbsp; use as needed but are not dedicated aircraft.&nbsp; China and India have done this for many years&nbsp;&nbsp; but have both now ordered dedicated aircraft&nbsp; – the 747-8 for China and the 777 for India. Of course, the most well-known use of government&nbsp; use is the United States Air Force-operated 747s.&nbsp;&nbsp; These are currently two heavily modified&nbsp; 747-200 aircraft (designated VC-25A),&nbsp;&nbsp; and have been in service since 1990. They will&nbsp; soon be replaced by two new 747-8 aircraft.&nbsp;&nbsp; In an effort to lower costs, these were&nbsp; aircraft originally ordered by Transaero.&nbsp;&nbsp; The planes will be modified&nbsp; instead of being newly built.&nbsp; Unlike many other countries, the interior details&nbsp; of Air Force One are well known and photographed.&nbsp;&nbsp; However, much of the defense and technical&nbsp; modifications, of course, remain classified.&nbsp; The main deck offers residence and office&nbsp; space for the President, followed by&nbsp;&nbsp; office and meeting space for staff and a large&nbsp; conference room. Behind all this are separate&nbsp;&nbsp; cabins for guests and the traveling media. To learn more about Air Force One and its history,&nbsp;&nbsp; check out our video “Air Force One:&nbsp; The Story of the Flying White House.” There have been only a few private owners of a&nbsp; 747. Even for large companies, it is simply too&nbsp;&nbsp; big and would rarely justify the operating costs.&nbsp; For corporate use, smaller aircraft, taking much&nbsp;&nbsp; smaller groups, are more practical. Private&nbsp; individuals likewise do not need the space,&nbsp;&nbsp; but as the largest private jet, the&nbsp; 747 definitely makes an impression!&nbsp; Not surprisingly, many private&nbsp; owners prefer to remain unnamed.&nbsp;&nbsp; But few examples have been publicized. Prince Al-Waleed bin Talal bin Abdulaziz&nbsp;&nbsp; al Saud is a member of the Saudi royal family but&nbsp; privately owns and operates aircraft. He purchased&nbsp;&nbsp; his 747 in 2003 for $485 million (for comparison,&nbsp; the US VC25A aircraft cost $325 million each).&nbsp; It is lavishly furnished with luxurious&nbsp; carpets, plenty of gold trim decoration,&nbsp;&nbsp; and even a large chandelier. There&nbsp; are decadent bedrooms and bathrooms,&nbsp;&nbsp; a dining area for 14, extensive lounge areas,&nbsp; and to top it all off – a throne for the Prince.&nbsp; The Prince planned to take his ambitions even&nbsp; further, with an order for a private A380 in 2007.&nbsp;&nbsp; This was designed but never developed, and&nbsp; the aircraft transferred to airline use.&nbsp; The three lift-connected decks (which included&nbsp; the cargo hold) would house... a car garage,&nbsp;&nbsp; Turkish bath, relaxation room with&nbsp; ground views projected on the floor,&nbsp;&nbsp; dining, and prayer rooms. And the upper deck would&nbsp; have five private en-suite apartments for guests. Meanwhile… There have been reports of the&nbsp; completion of a private 747-8 order from&nbsp;&nbsp; a Middle Eastern business. French firm Cabinet&nbsp; Alberto Pinto designed the interior. The extensive&nbsp;&nbsp; refit took four years and was completed in 2015. It features a master bedroom in the aircraft’s&nbsp;&nbsp; nose. There are several more private suites on&nbsp; the main deck, as well as office and lounge space.&nbsp;&nbsp; However, unlike the Prince’s jet, the interiors&nbsp; here were designed with simplicity in mind.&nbsp; The upper deck is used for a&nbsp; living room area, with bookshelves,&nbsp;&nbsp; televisions, and a children’s play area. In an interview with Altitudes Magazine,&nbsp;&nbsp; lead designer Pickardt explained&nbsp; the final result, saying...&nbsp; “The owner gave us carte blanche. He wasn’t&nbsp; looking for anything outrageously luxurious,&nbsp;&nbsp; with gold and diamonds. On the contrary he&nbsp; called for some simplicity, which in the end&nbsp;&nbsp; is indeed the true luxury. One can easily&nbsp; recognize a strong French touch, a tribute&nbsp;&nbsp; to French Art deco and to our most famous&nbsp; saddle makers. We used only noble materials,&nbsp;&nbsp; all of the best quality available of course, such&nbsp; as wood, (African Wakapou and Sycamore), stone,&nbsp;&nbsp; wool and silk fabrics, natural leathers.” Certainly proud of the design, he concludes with:&nbsp; “Such a project is a dream come true that happens&nbsp; only once or twice in a designer’s lifetime. In a&nbsp;&nbsp; way, this is a landmark in aviation history.&nbsp; Officials from the Boeing Company who visited&nbsp;&nbsp; the aircraft said that it may be one of the&nbsp; most beautiful large aircraft ever made!” The future for large private aircraft is&nbsp; interesting. There is a strong decline in the&nbsp;&nbsp; popularity and use of large four-engine jets. And&nbsp; Boeing has confirmed the end of 747 production,&nbsp;&nbsp; with the last two cargo 747-8s now ordered. But&nbsp; with increasing retirements from airline fleets,&nbsp;&nbsp; there could be the potential for&nbsp; more secondhand developments. The last 747-8 new build business jet order was&nbsp; in 2011, and there won’t be anymore now. Likewise,&nbsp;&nbsp; the A380 is also now out of production,&nbsp; with no private versions ever delivered.&nbsp; Twin-engine widebodies are&nbsp; now set to have their day.&nbsp;&nbsp; According to Boeing data, 14 787s and six 777s&nbsp; have now been ordered as business jets. And Boeing&nbsp;&nbsp; has released some exciting plans for a 777X BBJ. We have recently seen Germany take on two new&nbsp;&nbsp; A350 aircraft as state aircraft (to replace&nbsp; its A340s). And India has introduced the&nbsp;&nbsp; 777 as its first dedicated VIP aircraft. The Mexican government opted to go with&nbsp;&nbsp; the Boeing 787 for its new VIP transport.&nbsp; However, the current president believes&nbsp;&nbsp; this is an unnecessary luxury, and has put it&nbsp; up for sale. As yet, it hasn’t found a buyer. With more large aircraft retiring in 2020 and&nbsp; 2021, increasing second-hand availability could&nbsp;&nbsp; also affect the market. This is likely to&nbsp; be more of a factor in the A380 market,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially as its resale&nbsp; value has now dropped 50%.&nbsp; Retiring 747-400 aircraft are already nearing the&nbsp; end of service and are unlikely to see further VIP&nbsp;&nbsp; use. 747-8 aircraft are a possibility, but&nbsp; there are not many of them. We did see this&nbsp;&nbsp; with the new order from the US Air Force&nbsp; for replacement Air Force One aircraft.&nbsp; The 747 is a great aircraft, popular with&nbsp; airlines and private use – even if only limited.&nbsp;&nbsp; What other private uses do you think it&nbsp; would be suited for? And do you think any&nbsp;&nbsp; more will find a new life in second-hand&nbsp; use? Feel free to discuss in the comments.

In February of 2024, both engines suddenly failed as the Hop-A-Jet Flight 823 made its final approach to Naples just miles from the runway. The pilots tried landing on the interstate but crashed and both died. A flight attendant and two passengers managed to escape. Chief Wing Investigates reporter Chris Islander has combed through the new report today and joins us to break it all down. Chris. Well, Chris, this is the final 21-page report from the NTSB after 2 years of investigating. It says that the crash was the result of a series of failures. It said it started weeks before that final descent in Naples. February 9th, 2024, a cloud of black smoke rising from the southbound lanes of I-75. Hop-A-Jet Flight 823 was seconds from landing when both engines went silent. Lost an engine, some emergency. I'm making an emergency landing. Uh we're clear to land but we're not going to make the runway. Uh we've lost both engines. Now, more than 2 years later, the NTSB has the answer as to why. According to the final report, the culprit was corrosion. Because this plane was frequently based in Barbados and South Florida, it says salt accumulated in the engines taking what would become a deadly toll. Being a pilot, my my hat's off to those folks there. Uh they did the best they could. They didn't have any options at that point. >> Robert Joslin, a professor with Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University, reviewed the report. He says engine corrosion makes sense given where the plane was operating. That is very very real. Uh the military uh definitely corrosion control, you have whole departments dedicated to that. According to the NTSB, as the pilots reduced power to land, that salt air corrosion caused critical components to lock up. The engine stalled and at just 1,000 ft in the air, there was no time to recover. But the report reveals this wasn't the first sign of trouble. 25 days before the crash, the plane experienced hung starts, a known warning sign. This was uh highly improbable but but it happens. Yeah. Investigators found that while mechanics troubleshooted the issue, a key pressure test that it says could have detected that corrosion was never performed. Why? Because at the time, the manufacturer's manuals didn't require it. The NTSB is now calling that guidance inadequate. In the wake of this tragedy, the engine manufacturer has officially changed its protocols worldwide. Corrosion testing is no longer an option. It's now one of the first steps mechanics must take. While the NTSB does not assign blame, the report highlights the bravery of the crew. Pilots In Hoffman and Edward Murphy maneuvered the jet away from cars on the highway and the flight attendant managed to lead two passengers to safety through rear baggage door as the cabin filled with smoke. And that change in maintenance protocol seems to be the biggest takeaway for aviation safety. By moving that corrosion test to the front of the line, the NTSB believes that mechanics will catch this salt air build-up before it ever reaches a critical failure point again. For Wing Investigates, I'm Chris Islander. All right, we certainly hope so, Chris. Thank you. - The Economics of Flying Private: How NetJets Became the Uber of Private Jets: Kind: captions Language: en What does it actually cost to fly private? And why are more people doing it than ever before? Private aviation is a multibillion dollar business. And companies like NetJets are changing how the ultra wealthy move. Even in the smallest aircraft type in our program, you're talking today about an initial capital investment of more than $800,000 to buy a piece of an airplane. We have something like 40% of the Fortune 500 that are are NetJets customers. And as major commercial airlines are trying to win back premium customers, can commercial aviation companies successfully keep them? [Music] NetJets doesn't sell private jets. It sells fractions of jets. Customers buy a share as little as 116th of a specific aircraft model. That idea splitting jet ownership like a time share unlocked a new market. Today we operate nearly,00 aircraft which if you were comparing us to a commercial airline would make us the second or third largest on the planet. Unlike charter services, it owns and controls its fleet allowing tighter control over quality scheduling and safety. The average NetJets customer flies with us for about 75 to 100 hours per year. Our typical customer will be somebody who is a principal of a privately held company. We have something like 40% of the Fortune 500 that are our NetJets customers. Flying with NetJets doesn't come cheap. A fractional ownership share on a light jet like the Phenom 300 starts at around $850,000 upfront, plus about $325,000 per year in operating costs for 50 flight hours. For those who want a more affordable and flexible option, the NetJets card starts at $215,000 for 25 hours annually. That $215,000 jet card, that's the cost of four down payments on the average American home. Within the shared ownership model, we really have three cost components for the customer. You have the initial capital investment, which is a onetime investment where somebody's buying a piece of the airplane and then they pay a monthly management fee. If you owned your own airplane, you'd have pilot salaries, you need to have mechanic salaries, and you'd have hanger rent. So in our model, you're sharing that across the other members of the program. And then every time you fly, we bill you the variable hourly costs associated with flying that trip from A to B, which covers things like the fuel expense of the aircraft, the ground charges and the handling fees and the things that that would go into operating an airplane from A to B. That business model caught the attention of Warren Buffett. And in 1998, Berkshire Hathaway bought NetJets and has held on to it since. You see in the luxury travel segment overall, people are making more of an investment in experiences than in things. That certainly has been a tailwind for us. You know, if I'm being honest, the commercial experience has not gotten any better over the last several years, and that has driven more people to want to fly netjets. During the pandemic, demand for private air travel soared as people feared crowded airports and packed planes. In the US, business aviation traffic in 2021 hit record highs, surpassing the 2019 levels, and in 2021 saw over 3.3 million private jets worldwide, a 7% increase over preandemic levels. Only about 10% of the people who met the financial, you know, norms or averages of private aviation were actually flying privately. and CO certainly unlocked some portion of that addressable market. And then the big question was would they go back to the airlines when the pandemic subsided and you know masks came off and vaccines went in arms. After the initial co boom many private airline companies saw slumps in profits but in Q1 of 2025 Berkshire Hathaway reported a profit bump in its service division which it contributed to its aviation services. For NetJets, we're operating at 40 45% larger than we were in 2019. So, we've maintained record retention rates. Major commercial airlines are trying to claw back premium travelers by rolling out redesigned business class cabins, faster boarding, and high-end lounges. But for some flyers, the best perks aren't enough. Anonymity is key to our business. One of the appeals of flying netjets versus owning your own airplane is that you're virtually untraceable. Whether you have concerns about appearances or whether you have concerns about safety, netjets can provide that in a way that that you can't get owning your own airplane. As the demand for private flights increases, so does the visibility of their impact. Each trip emits many times more carbon emissions per passenger than a commercial trip, and that's drawn growing scrutiny, especially as more celebrities and CEOs are called out for frequent short hall trips. When Taylor Swift's private jet usage was tracked online in 2023, it sparked a wave of backlash and highlighted the growing divide between convenience and climate responsibility. Now with the business booming, even the industry's biggest players are under pressure to address the carbon footprint. Sustainability is at the forefront for our industry right now. And the way that we're addressing it at NetIts is we are the largest buyer of sustainable aviation fuel in the industry. We offer carbon offset programs for our customers. We offset all of our own internal company flying. So anything that we do for maintenance flights or training flights or anything like that, we we buy the carbon credits to offset. As commercial travel faces continued pressure from rising costs to growing delays, demand for more efficient discrete options isn't likely to vanish. Private aviation remains a niche but profitable business fueled by clients who value control more than cost.

- Government shutdown reaches day 37: Bozeman airport braces for potential flight reductions: Kind: captions Language: en The current government shutdown is now the longest in US history and during its 37th day. Now that's 37 days where federal employees such as TSA agents and air traffic controllers haven't been paid. So I headed over to the Boseman Yellowstone International Airport to see if that lack of paycheck is having any effects. >> May I have your attention, please? >> I walked around and spoke to folks who just got off a plane. To my surprise, many people had this same response. >> Absolutely no problems at all. Well, uh, it was busy in the air, but everything goes very well. >> It was smooth as usual, so no issues. >> But all of these folks were on the same flight from Chicago. The answer changed a bit when I met Jennifer, who just got in from Denver. >> I was there really early at 5:00 a.m. in anticipation of not knowing what the TSA lines were looking like. >> What Jennifer found when she got to the airport, it was starting to back up even at that hour. And also, um, I noticed a lot of reporters that were out were probably doing the same thing as you. >> And that's because in a recent press conference, US Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy announced starting Friday, there will be a 10% reduction in US air traffic at 40 major airports. What that could look like for BZN, >> those generally are where our flights go to. If one of our flights is one of that 10%, then obviously it could have some impact on our passengers. >> In the transportation secretary's press conference, he stated this 10% reduction is due to staffing shortages. Currently, airports across the US are 2,000 air traffic controllers short. According to the Department of Transportation, there are more than 13,000 air traffic controllers currently working without pay, leading many to quit, call in sick, or find other jobs. But we're a little different in terms of the air traffic control in that we have a contracted air traffic control tower and they are still getting paid at this time. >> For TSA employees, they're not as fortunate. >> Being able to have them come to work every day and process passengers through the checkpoint is asking a lot of them when they're not getting paid. >> Brian Springer, chief executive officer of the airport, tells me at BZN they haven't experienced employees quitting or calling in sick. >> But how long can that last? I mean, you know, people have to make decisions for their livelihood. >> Springer has worked at BZN for nearly 30 years. He was there for the last government shutdown that ended in 2019. He hopes this air traffic reduction might be the beginning of the end. >> The last government shutdown, things were at a standstill until people started getting affected and air travel is one of those things that, you know, could start impacting people. in Bosezeman. Cassidy Powers, MTN News.

- The Economics of Private Jets: Kind: captions Language: en This video was made possible by Curiosity Stream. Watch over 2,400 documentaries for free for a month at CuriosityStream.com/Wendover. Private jets represent an inconceivable level of opulence. If the average American were to spend their entire year’s salary to charter a Gulfstream G550 from New York, they would just barely make it to Utah, and yet, there’s a class of people who use these planes to fly not just from New York to Utah, but rather routes like New York to Beijing. There’s a class of people who will spend tens or hundreds of thousands of dollars on just one single flight. Now, there are a few reasons this is strange beyond just the sheer price of things. Chartering that Gulfstream from London to Dubai, for example, you’d end up paying about $55,000 at rack rate. Meanwhile, if you were to fly Emirates First Class, which is just, if not more opulent, you could fly between the cities twenty times for the same price. What’s even stranger about this kind of expense is that businesses, which are intended to maximize profits for their shareholders, are able to justify this enormous expense as worthwhile. So, when is it that paying $8,000 or more an hour to fly makes money? There’s a pretty simple way of figuring this out. Out of the 8,760 hours in a year, the average CEO works 2,716 of them. For a CEO that’s paid $1 million a year, that makes an hour of their time worth $368. Among America’s largest companies, though, the average CEO earns $15.6 million. That makes their hour worth $5,750. For the most part, though, private jets fly about the same speed as commercial planes so when flying a route like London to Dubai, the time savings come at the airports on each end. It comes from being able to arrive, get on a plane, and fly—rather than having to navigate one’s way through a busy terminal for a fixed flight time. But still, flying private versus commercial from London to Dubai would save, at most, about three hours in airport time. With the cost of $55,000 for the flight, that would mean the CEO’s time would have to be worth $18,300 per hour. That wouldn’t be true until they earned $50 million a year—a salary earned only by the upper echelon of CEO’s. But the truth is that, for the most part, private jets do not make economic sense when flying routes with plenty of commercial service like London to Dubai. One of the larger corporate jet fleets out there belongs to Walmart. Now, this might come as a surprise considering that this is a company so focused on keeping costs low. You see, Walmart is headquartered in Bentonville, AR—a relatively small city of 50,000. Their airport does have a surprising amount of service for such a small city with flights all the way to Los Angeles and New York, largely propped up the company’s traffic, but for the higher ups, commercial doesn’t cut it. That’s why the company has a fleet of 20 corporate jets—the largest of any American company. These are most frequently flown by the company’s Regional Vice Presidents who are in charge of a specific area of the country and will have to make frequent store visits within this region. The company apparently has a goal that nobody spends a night away from Bentonville—they want as many trips as possible to be day trips. Now, let’s say that one of these executive vice presidents needs to take a trip to three stores—one in Rock Springs, Wyoming; one in Spokane, Washington; and the last in Great Falls, Montana. Getting to Rock Springs requires a seven hour itinerary through Denver that would get this executive in at 9:30 pm therefore already requiring an overnight stop. Then, the next day, they would do their store visit in the morning and, as there are only two flights a day from Rock Springs, they would have to wait until 4:50 pm to catch a flight back to Denver then another one to Spokane, getting in at 8:30 pm local time, therefore requiring another overnight stop. The next morning they would do their site visit, but once again, flight schedules dictate that the first itinerary to Great Falls would leave at 5:05 pm through Salt Lake City, getting in at 10:04 pm local time, thereby requiring another overnight stop. Following the next morning’s store visit, this executive would catch a noon flight to Denver and, after a three hour layover, another to finally get into Bentonville at 8pm. These three store visits would therefore take up four whole working days, but what if this executive flew private? Leaving at 9am, the first flight direct to Rock Springs would take an hour and 45 minutes getting in, with the hour’s time change, at 9:45 am local time. After a two hour store visit, the plane would take off again at 11:45 am, flying an hour and 15 minutes to Spokane, getting in at noon local time. After another two hour store visit, the plane would take off at 2 pm for a quick 45 minute flight to Great Falls, getting in at 3:45 pm local time. After a final two hours at this store, the plane would take off its final time at 5:45 pm bound for Bentonville. 2 hours and 15 minutes of flight time later, it would land at 9 pm local time, exactly 12 hours after leaving. What was a four day trip on commercial flights becomes a day trip on private, and that’s why Walmart decided private jets are worth it for them. It’s all about valuing the time of their employees and they’ve determined that, even for the relatively low level vice presidents, their time is valuable enough that it’s worth flying them private. For example, one of the aircraft Walmart owns and operates is the Learjet 45. It costs the company about $4 per mile to operate this aircraft including crew, fuel, insurance, maintenance, and all other variable costs. Therefore, the 2,900 miles flown on that day-trip to the north-east would cost them about $11,600. Saving three days, that places a value of $3,900 per day which means that, assuming the executive onboard works every single one of the 260 workdays per year, they would have to make almost exactly $1 million per year for this private jet ride to be worth it to Walmart—an amount within the realm of possibility for upper management at such a large company. Of course, that’s not factoring in the alternative option’s hotel, food, and airline transport costs which would likely sum in the thousands and it’s also assuming there’s just one passenger. If the plane were to be filled to its maximum capacity of nine, each passenger would only need to be paid $111,000 per year for the expense to be worth it to the company which is less than an average Walmart store manager makes. Now, there’s one other case where private jets can make economic sense over flying commercial. Let’s say Walmart was looking to expand into the Philippines. Flying business class, it would cost a minimum of $5,000 roundtrip per person, require three stops, and take over 26 hours to get from Bentonville to Manila. Flying private, though, a long range jet like the Bombardier Global 7500 could make it there non-stop, in just 15 hours, carrying 19 of the company’s top executives. Since the company does not own this type of jet, it would likely charter one at a cost of about $10,000 an hour, or $150,000 for the trip. While the cost of commercial airfare is less than this, assuming the CEO, who makes $24 million a year, is onboard, the value of the eleven hours of his time saved is worth $97,000—clearly tilting the math in favor of the private jet. The general phenomenon of globalization has been great for the private jet market as businesses need to travel to far off places like this. Especially as companies outsource manufacturing and other operations into developing countries, which don't necessary have much air service, many companies have determined that private jets are the best way to get where they need to go. But despite this, the private aviation industry was hit hard but the global financial crisis and still has not fully recovered. While part of it was genuine cost cutting, businesses also wanted to show that they were doubling down on luxuries by getting rid of their jets, even if they could make economic sense in some cases. It was all about optics and nowadays, these jets are coming with poorer and poorer optics, for good reason. Private jets are truly horrendous for the environment. If one were to fly that Bombardier Global 7500, the one that could make it from Bentonville to Manila, with just one passenger onboard, the jet would make it only to South Dakota before that passenger’s carbon footprint exceeded that of the average person in one year. Increasingly, these jets are even being used for purposes that cannot be justified economically. Since 2013, there has been about a 10% increase among Fortune 100 executives of using their company’s corporate jets for personal, leisure purposes. They apparently justify this by saying that, in case of a work emergency, they might need to get back to the office quickly and commercial air travel could hinder that. Firms that include this as a perk for their executives, according to one set of research, under-perform against the market average in terms of shareholder returns by about 4% each Of course, the real reason some companies might have private jets is not because it makes economic sense, because it quite often doesn’t. It’s because the people who decide whether the firm will use these are the very people that will use them. In many instances, the explanation is not economic, it’s societal. If that’s you or if you’d like that to be you, you should absolutely be subscribed to CuriosityStream. That’s because they have a library of over 2,400 documentaries and non-fiction titles that you can watch whenever, wherever. It’s available on the web, Apple TV, Roku, Android, iOS, and plenty of other platforms. One series they have that I would highly recommend is Innovation Nation with Mo Rocca who looks at new inventions in each episode and explains the stories behind them.   8. @theophrastus3.056: A good analysis for the most part. However the initial calculations (later corrected) are for a single CEO making solo trips. In reality, he’s usually accompanied by several other execs, also making top dollar. 
       The value of a corporate jet doing in one day what would take several days on the airlines is spot on. However, the flight time on the private aircraft is also work time. There’s no privacy or a cabin setup that makes this possible on the airlines.

  11. @jeremyleonbarlow: The economics are complex. I mean if you are living in Los Angeles and you have 6 or 7 buddies and you all want to fly to Vegas for the weekend, you can rent a light jet to fly you all collectively to Vegas and back for about the same cost as a commercial round trip ticket, give or take a couple of hundred dollars each per ticket. I mean the complexity of commercial ticket pricing gives a lot of wiggle room, but a realistic rental price for 8 friends flying LA to Vegas round trip on a light jet is between $500 and $600 each.

Renting a long range heavy jet or super mid-size private jet for a lengthy trip between cities for a business isn't just about the time savings for a single person in management typically. I mean a 15 or 20 seat private business jet probably has at a minimum ten people flying on it, so you need to factor in the price differential between X number of tickets and the costs of chartering or using a company owned jet, and what is more given fixed costs of an aircraft, even with high up front costs, owning a jet might be more economical still. This is because there are ways to improve the value of an owned aircraft which allows you to factor more than just the value of those folks' time savings at aurports. You must add to that calculus the fact that many businesses and individuals also subsidize company or personal travel on a private jet by triple net leasing their owned jet to a charter service with a deal that allows them 10 or more hours per month of usage at cost. This type of leasing arrangement is often through a service that allows them to use a network of jets in the charter service's fleet. This helps obscure travel by the company, protecting proprietary information in many instances better than traditional private ownership. I mean Taylor Swift and Elon Musk's tail numbers were being tracked as I recall and Sergei Brin and Larry Page decided chartering was more economical for this exact reason. Competitors knowing your next move by tracking where you flew is not a good thing, but owning and using a networked leaseback arrangement can be more economical in addition to obscuring your travel.

These leasing arrangements maximize cash flow on the jet as an asset to both the charter operator and the lessor by allowing the jets to keep moving and avoid a fixed base of operation. This maximizes charter revenue. This reduces the costs further for the owner of the jet by spreading fixed costs over more hours of usage and offloading those costs onto chartered guests. I mean with amortization and depreciation, a company which might have spent $7.5 million flying private over the course of five years, after the savings associated with the right charter leaseback arrangement, could have in fact spent just $360k for the same 600 hours of flight time. At what becomes $600 per hour for the flight time in such an arrangement, owning a private company jet makes a whole lot more sense.

Now it isn't always going to allow the company or individual to reduce costs that much, as some unexpected maintenance items might raise costs. I mean one cracked windshield could costs tens of thousands of dollars to replace, not to mention lost revenue from the loss of flight time while waiting for the repair, but it limits downside of ownership, and can still make flying private far more economical than traditional chartering. Of course chartering still might be cost effective for businesses or individuals who fly less frequently because of the value of time savings alone. This is why high usage owners can subsidize their travel costs.

- NetJets vs Flexjet – Which Private Jet Service Is Worth It in 2026?: Kind: captions Language: en Hey everyone, thinking about flying private in 2025. Let's break down two big names in private jet travel. NetJets and Flexjet. We'll explain each then compare them to help you pick the best one for you. Let's dive in. First, NetJets. NetJets is the biggest name in private jets. Started in 1964 and owned by Warren Buffett's Birkshshire Hathaway. They have over 800 planes from small jets like the Phenom 300 to big ones like the Bombardier Global 8000. They offer fractional ownership where you buy a share of a jet for 50 plus hours a year or jet cars for 25 hours blocks. NetJet is known for reliability, a huge network and top safety standards, but it's pricey and their jet card has 45 pick days where you need to book far in advance. Next, Flexjet. Flexjet launched in 1995 is the second largest player with about 300 planes including Gulfream and Embrier Jets. They also do fractional ownership and jet carts, but they focus on luxury and customization. Their red label program gives you dedicated crews and fancy cabins. Flexjet has fewer pig days, only 10, and shorter notice for bookings. They're a bit cheaper than NetJets and offer cool perks like helicopter transfers. So, how do they stack up? NetJets has a bigger fleet and global reach, perfect for frequent flyers who need flexibility across the world. Their safety records and experience are hard to beat, but you'll pay more and pick day restrictions can be a hassle. Flexjet shines for those who want a luxury experience with custom touches and lower costs. Their smaller fleet means less availability, but fewer pick days and faster upgrades make it great for shorter trips or style focused travelers, which is worth it. If you fly a lot internationally and want the most options, go with netjets. If you value luxury, flexibility, and saving some cash, Flexjet's your pick. It depends on your needs. Both are awesome. Thanks for watching. Fly smart in 2025. Like and subscribe for more videos.
